title: Tracking Missingness
concept: Adding binary flags (indicators) to show where data was missing helps models learn patterns from the missingness itself.

exercises.prompt: Recap: When is dropping an entire column of data preferred?

options.text: When the column is mostly missing values and cannot be reliably filled
When the column contains name values
When the column is completely filled with correct numbers

exercises.explain: Correct! If a column is mostly empty, imputation is just guessing, so dropping it is safer.

cards.title: The clue in the empty space
cards.body: If a patient leaves the 'heart rate history' blank on a medical form, is it just random? Or did they skip it because they don't have heart issues? The absence is information!

cards.title: Missing Indicators
cards.body: A missing indicator is a new binary column (0 or 1) added to a dataset, flagging exactly which values were originally missing before they were imputed.

cards.title: Labeling the patch
cards.body: If you patch a hole in a wall with plaster (imputation), it looks clean. But if you place a small sticky note on it saying 'patched here', everyone still knows there was a hole.

cards.title: The 'Age_Was_Missing' column
cards.body: If you fill missing ages with the average (30), you add a column: Age_Was_Missing. It holds 1 if the age was blank, and 0 if it was real. The AI can see the difference!

cards.title: Missing isn't always random
cards.body: People assume data goes missing by random luck. But rich people often hide their income, and healthy people skip medical tests. Tracking missingness lets the model learn these human behaviors.

cards.title: Imputed vs Imputed + Flagged
cards.body: Why simple imputation isn't always enough:

left.text:
Filled value is 30
AI treats it as real '30'
No way to know it was a guess

right.text:
Filled value is 30
New column flags 'Was_Missing = 1'
AI learns a guess was made

exercises.prompt: Why should we add a missing indicator column when imputing data?

options.text: To let the model know which values were estimated so it can learn from the missingness
To make the database file larger and more secure
To automatically delete the rows that were filled
To change all 1s in the data to 0s

exercises.explain: Yes! The fact that data was missing is often a useful clue for the model's predictions.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Data is always missing completely at random, so tracking missingness is pointless.
exercises.explain: Correct! Data is often missing for systematic reasons (e.g. people skipping questions they find sensitive).

exercises.sentence: A missing indicator column uses ___ values like 0 and 1.
exercises.explain: Yes, a binary indicator (0 for present, 1 for originally missing) is used to flag the data.

exercises.prompt: Which one is a missing indicator column?

options.text: A column named 'Income' with filled-in averages
A list of all missing values removed from the file

exercises.explain: Exactly! It is a separate column flagging the presence of missing values in the original data.

exercises.prompt: You are training an AI to predict credit card default. You notice many high-income applicants skip the 'Current Debt' question. If you impute their debt with the average without flagging it, what happens?

options.text: The model treats them like average debtors, missing the signal that their silence might indicate high debt
The model automatically calculates their exact debt anyway
The applicants' credit scores immediately drop

exercises.explain: Spot on! Without a flag, the model loses the signal hidden in their choice to hide the data. Now, let's look at the ultimate trap: data leakage!
